When individuals attempt to juggle too many responsibilities, they are more likely to rush through tasks, sacrificing depth for the sake of ticking items off a list. For instance, a student who joins three clubs, plays on a sports team, and volunteers at a local charity may gain breadth of experience but miss the opportunity to make a meaningful impact in any one area. Additionally, relationships with family and friends suffer when every moment is scheduled, as genuine connection requires time and presence—commodities that busyness often deprives us of.

Nevertheless, it is important to distinguish between purposeful activity and mere busyness. Engaging in meaningful pursuits, such as pursuing a passion or supporting one’s community, can foster fulfillment and personal development. The problem arises when being busy becomes an end in itself, prioritized over intentional living. Instead of striving to fill every hour, students and adults alike should aim for balance, setting aside time for rest, reflection, and genuine engagement with the world around them.

Ultimately, while a busy lifestyle is sometimes inevitable, it should not be idolized. The true measure of success lies not in the number of activities one can cram into a day, but in the richness of one’s experiences and the authenticity of one’s connections. By embracing intentionality over busyness, individuals can cultivate a more meaningful, healthy, and rewarding life.

In today’s fast-paced world, being busy has become a badge of honor, a sign that one is ambitious, productive, and successful. However, while perpetual busyness is often celebrated—especially among students striving to excel academically and socially—it is crucial to recognize that being constantly busy is not inherently beneficial. Instead, true success and fulfillment come from balance, intentionality, and the ability to prioritize wisely.

First and foremost, it is important to question the assumption that being busy equates to being productive or accomplished. Teenagers, in particular, are vulnerable to the pressures of juggling schoolwork, sports, part-time jobs, and social lives. By embracing periods of rest and setting realistic boundaries, students can safeguard their health and sustain their motivation over time.

Admittedly, some might argue that being busy teaches valuable skills such as time management and discipline. It is not the sheer quantity of tasks that develops character, but the thoughtful choice of which responsibilities to assume and which to decline. By learning to distinguish between what is urgent and what is important, students gain not only efficiency but also wisdom.

Ultimately, it is not the number of commitments that defines us, but the intention with which we pursue them. Rather than idolizing busyness, we ought to celebrate balance, depth, and authenticity. Only then can we unlock our fullest potential—both as students and as individuals capable of leading rich, fulfilling lives.

In today’s fast-paced society, the state of being busy is often regarded as an indicator of productivity and success. However, I believe that the glorification of constant busyness is misguided, as it can undermine both personal well-being and genuine accomplishment. While it is true that staying engaged in various activities may foster a sense of purpose and personal growth, it is essential to recognize that being busy does not necessarily equate to being effective or fulfilled. Instead, mindful engagement and deliberate rest are far more valuable than simply filling every moment with activity.

First and foremost, the relentless pursuit of busyness frequently leads to stress and burnout. Teenagers, for instance, are often encouraged to participate in a multitude of extracurriculars, advanced classes, and volunteer opportunities in order to build an impressive resume. Although these experiences can be rewarding, the pressure to continuously “do more” can leave students overwhelmed, exhausted, and even disenchanted with activities they once enjoyed. This phenomenon is supported by numerous studies linking chronic over-scheduling with increased anxiety and declining mental health among adolescents. Thus, the mere accumulation of tasks, without regard to their significance or personal resonance, can be ultimately self-defeating.

Furthermore, the obsession with being busy often distracts individuals from meaningful reflection and creativity. When every hour is jam-packed with obligations, there is little opportunity to pause, contemplate, and derive insights from one’s experiences. Genuine innovation—whether in the arts, sciences, or personal development—often emerges during moments of quietude, when the mind is free to wander and synthesize new ideas. By contrast, a calendar overflowing with back-to-back commitments leaves scant room for the sort of imaginative thinking that leads to breakthroughs.

Admittedly, there is value in being industrious and pursuing one’s ambitions with vigor. However, true achievement is not measured by how busy one appears, but by the quality and impact of one’s efforts. By learning to prioritize meaningful engagements and carving out intentional time for rest, individuals can achieve a healthier balance that enables both productivity and personal satisfaction. Ultimately, it is the thoughtful allocation of one’s time—not the frantic rush to fill every moment—that leads to genuine success and well-being.

In sum, while being busy may be celebrated in contemporary culture, it is not inherently virtuous. It is essential to resist the pressure to equate busyness with worth and instead cultivate a life that balances purposeful action with restorative pause. Only then can we truly thrive, both as students and as individuals.

In today’s fast-paced and hyperconnected world, being busy has become almost a badge of honor among high school students and adults alike. However, while some argue that constant busyness is merely an inevitable byproduct of ambition and productivity, I contend that a culture of perpetual busyness is ultimately detrimental to individual well-being, the quality of our work, and our ability to form meaningful connections with others.

First and foremost, constantly being busy erodes our mental and physical health, even if we are initially motivated by noble intentions. It is easy to conflate being busy with being successful, but an overcrowded schedule often leads to chronic stress, sleep deprivation, and burnout. Furthermore, when students juggle demanding classes, extracurricular activities, and part-time jobs, they rarely have time to reflect, recharge, or pursue creative interests. For example, a student who participates in ten clubs but never has time to truly invest in any one of them will likely miss out on the profound growth and satisfaction that comes from mastery. In contrast, focusing on a select few meaningful pursuits allows for genuine expertise, skill development, and a more rewarding sense of accomplishment. By choosing to be less busy, we open ourselves to spontaneous moments of joy and connection that enrich our lives far beyond anything a packed calendar can provide.

In contemporary society, being busy is frequently equated with being successful, productive, and even virtuous. Many students, professionals, and parents wear their busyness like a badge of honor, believing that a packed schedule signifies purpose and achievement. However, while the state of being busy can sometimes reflect ambition and drive, it is neither inherently beneficial nor sustainable. Instead, an overcommitment to busyness can undermine genuine productivity, mental health, and meaningful relationships. For these reasons, society should rethink its obsession with being busy and prioritize deliberate, purposeful engagement over relentless activity.

First and foremost, being busy does not necessarily mean being productive. It is entirely possible to fill one’s day with countless obligations and tasks, yet accomplish little of real value. For example, a student might join every club available, take on multiple advanced classes, and volunteer for various projects, thinking that such involvement will impress colleges or future employers. Nevertheless, spreading oneself too thin often leads to superficial engagement rather than deep, meaningful achievement. Studies have shown that multitasking decreases overall efficiency and increases the likelihood of errors. In other words, those who constantly rush from one responsibility to another rarely have time to think critically, reflect meaningfully, or excel at any single pursuit.

Moreover, the glorification of busyness can take a significant toll on mental and physical health. Chronic stress, sleep deprivation, and anxiety are increasingly common among those who are perpetually overscheduled. When individuals feel compelled to always be “doing,” they frequently neglect their own well-being and sacrifice opportunities for relaxation and self-care. For instance, high school students who are constantly occupied with extracurriculars and homework often report feelings of burnout and exhaustion. Instead of fostering growth and resilience, excessive busyness can lead to diminished motivation and, in severe cases, long-term psychological harm.

Finally, a culture of ceaseless activity undermines the quality of our relationships and our ability to connect with others. Meaningful relationships require time, attention, and presence—qualities that are difficult to provide when one’s mind is preoccupied with the next item on an endless to-do list. By prioritizing quantity of activity over quality of experience, we risk losing sight of what truly enriches our lives: genuine human connection, reflection, and joy.

In conclusion, while being busy is often celebrated as a sign of industriousness, it is vital to recognize its limitations and potential dangers. Rather than striving to fill every moment with activity, we should aim for purposeful engagement—choosing commitments that align with our values and allow us to flourish both personally and collectively. By doing so, we can achieve a more balanced, fulfilling, and meaningful life.

In today’s fast-paced society, where achievement is lauded and productivity is idolized, many people wear their busyness as a badge of honor. Yet, while being busy is often perceived as a virtue, it is essential to examine whether constant occupation truly benefits our lives or, instead, erodes our well-being and sense of fulfillment. I contend that being perpetually busy is not inherently advantageous; rather, it can hinder creativity, damage mental health, and prevent us from cultivating meaningful relationships.

To begin with, a schedule overflowing with obligations and tasks stifles the creativity that is vital for genuine innovation and personal growth. When every moment is accounted for, the mind is denied the freedom to wander, reflect, and make connections between disparate ideas—an essential process for original thought. For instance, history’s most creative minds, from Albert Einstein to J.K. Rowling, attributed some of their best ideas to periods of leisure or idle reflection. Therefore, an obsession with constant activity can paradoxically reduce one’s capacity to think deeply and inventively.

Furthermore, the glorification of busyness exacts a significant toll on mental health. When individuals push themselves relentlessly, often in pursuit of external validation or fear of falling behind, they risk succumbing to burnout, anxiety, and depression. According to the World Health Organization, chronic stress—much of which stems from overloaded schedules—has become a global epidemic. Ironically, the very drive to accomplish more can ultimately render people less effective, as fatigue and mental exhaustion erode both motivation and efficiency. In this light, it becomes clear that an unbalanced preoccupation with busyness is detrimental, not commendable.

Perhaps most importantly, being incessantly busy undermines the development of rich, meaningful relationships. Deep connections require time, attention, and presence—commodities that are in short supply for those who are always rushing from one commitment to the next. While it may be tempting to fill every hour with activities that promise academic or professional success, neglecting family and friends can lead to a profound sense of isolation and regret in the long term. After all, at life’s end, few people wish they had spent more time working or checking off to-do lists; instead, they cherish memories forged with loved ones.

In summary, although being busy is often equated with success, it is, in reality, a double-edged sword. True fulfillment arises not from relentless activity, but from striking a balance—allowing space for creativity, safeguarding mental well-being, and nurturing relationships. Society would be far better served by valuing intentional living over constant motion.

Through this process, they develop critical thinking and problem-solving abilities that serve them well in adulthood. If these students were instead to avoid busyness for fear of overwhelm, they would forfeit the valuable experiences that shape them into adaptable and resourceful individuals.

Moreover, being busy necessitates effective time management. Unlike those who have few obligations, busy individuals must constantly plan, prioritize, and execute tasks efficiently. These skills, honed through necessity, are indispensable in both academic and professional realms. While some critics assert that busyness leads to stress and burnout, it is important to recognize that the key lies not in avoiding busyness, but in learning to manage it wisely. By striking a balance and setting boundaries, people can reap the benefits of an active schedule without sacrificing their mental health. In fact, the satisfaction derived from accomplishing a well-organized day’s work often outweighs the temporary relief of leisure.

Finally, being busy imbues life with a sense of direction and fulfillment. Having meaningful tasks and responsibilities to occupy one’s time can prevent the aimlessness and boredom that frequently accompany prolonged idleness. When individuals immerse themselves in purposeful activities—whether studying for an exam, volunteering, or pursuing creative projects—they are less likely to dwell on negative thoughts or succumb to inertia. Consequently, busyness, far from being a burden, can be a source of joy and motivation.

In conclusion, while excessive busyness without reflection can undoubtedly be harmful, embracing a busy lifestyle with intention and balance can lead to profound personal development, mastery of essential skills, and a deeper sense of satisfaction. Rather than shunning busyness, we should recognize it as a catalyst for growth and self-discovery.

In today’s fast-paced society, being busy is often celebrated as a hallmark of ambition and productivity. From students juggling extracurricular activities to adults balancing demanding careers, the culture of constant busyness permeates nearly every aspect of modern life. However, despite the prevailing belief that being busy equates to being successful, I contend that excessive busyness is ultimately detrimental, both to individuals and to society. By glorifying relentless activity, we sacrifice mental well-being, the quality of our work, and the depth of our relationships.

To begin with, the relentless pursuit of busyness undermines mental and emotional health. While some may argue that staying occupied staves off boredom and fosters accomplishment, chronic busyness often leads to stress, anxiety, and burnout. According to the American Psychological Association, rates of anxiety and depression among teenagers and adults have risen in parallel with the increasing demands of school and work. When individuals fill every moment with tasks, they deny themselves opportunities for reflection and relaxation, both of which are essential for maintaining a balanced mind. Thus, the very state that is supposed to make us successful can, in reality, diminish our overall well-being.

Additionally, busyness frequently comes at the expense of quality. In academia, for example, students who overcommit themselves to numerous clubs, sports, and AP classes may find that they lack the time or energy to truly excel in any one area. Rather than developing a deep understanding of a subject or meaningful connections with mentors, they simply check off boxes on a resume. This phenomenon is not limited to students; professionals often multitask to the point of diminishing returns, where work becomes rushed and mistakes increase. Therefore, while being busy may create the illusion of productivity, it more often results in mediocrity and superficial achievement.

Moreover, the ceaseless hustle erodes the fabric of our relationships. When individuals prioritize schedules over people, they miss out on genuine human connection. Meaningful relationships require time, presence, and attention—commodities that are often in short supply for the perpetually busy. Family dinners, deep conversations, and shared experiences are sacrificed for the sake of squeezing in one more activity or assignment. Over time, this neglect can lead to feelings of isolation and disconnection, problems which are already alarmingly prevalent in our hyperconnected world.

In conclusion, although society prizes being busy as a virtue, the costs far outweigh the benefits. Rather than glorifying an overloaded schedule, we should advocate for a more balanced approach to life—one that values quality over quantity, fosters well-being, and prioritizes meaningful connections. True success lies not in how much we can do, but in how well we can live.

In today’s fast-paced world, being busy is often perceived as a badge of honor, a symbol of productivity, and an essential ingredient for success. However, while relentless busyness is sometimes glamorized, I firmly believe that being busy is, in fact, beneficial—both for individuals and society—when one’s efforts are meaningful and balanced. Embracing a busy lifestyle not only fosters personal growth but also cultivates valuable skills and fuels collective progress.

First and foremost, staying busy with purposeful activities enables people to develop discipline and time-management skills. When someone manages a full schedule—be it with academic pursuits, extracurricular activities, or part-time employment—they are compelled to prioritize and organize their time efficiently. For instance, a student who juggles challenging coursework alongside sports or volunteering learns to set goals, eliminate procrastination, and meet deadlines. These are not only useful in high school but are essential life skills that prepare individuals for the demands of adulthood. Without the structure that a busy schedule provides, many may find themselves succumbing to laziness or aimlessness, which ultimately hinders personal achievement.

Moreover, engaging in various undertakings broadens one’s horizons and fosters adaptability, a trait increasingly vital in our rapidly changing world. By participating in diverse activities, individuals are exposed to new perspectives, ideas, and challenges that sharpen their problem-solving abilities and resilience. For example, a busy student who participates in debate, community service, and science club encounters situations that demand creativity, collaboration, and empathy. These experiences not only enrich academic learning but also nurture well-rounded, socially conscious citizens.

Admittedly, some critics argue that being busy can lead to burnout and stress, especially when taken to unhealthy extremes. However, this risk underscores the need for balance, not for rejecting busyness altogether. When individuals choose activities that align with their passions and values, busyness becomes invigorating rather than exhausting. Furthermore, learning to manage stress is a crucial life lesson. Encouraging a balanced yet active lifestyle teaches young people to recognize their limits, seek support, and make wise choices about their commitments.

In conclusion, while unmitigated busyness can be detrimental, a well-managed, purpose-driven busy life unlocks a wealth of benefits. It instills discipline, builds adaptability, and prepares individuals to make meaningful contributions to society. Therefore, rather than shying away from being busy, we should embrace it—wisely and intentionally—as a path toward personal fulfillment and collective advancement.

In contemporary society, “being busy” has become something of a badge of honor. Many individuals, especially students, feel compelled to fill every available moment with activities—clubs, sports, rigorous coursework, part-time jobs, and volunteer work—believing that a packed schedule is the hallmark of ambition and productivity. While some argue that being busy is essential for personal growth and achievement, I contend that constant busyness is overrated and, in fact, detrimental to both well-being and authentic success. Rather than glorifying frenetic activity, we should learn to value balance, reflection, and purposeful engagement.

First and foremost, the ceaseless pursuit of busyness often leads to stress and burnout. Numerous studies have shown that chronic stress undermines mental and physical health, resulting in anxiety, sleep disturbances, and even weakened immune systems. When students, in particular, are encouraged to take on excessive responsibilities, they may sacrifice sleep, leisure, and meaningful social connections just to maintain the illusion of productivity. Although busyness is often equated with dedication, it can actually impede learning by leaving insufficient time for rest and deep reflection, both of which are essential for processing new information and fostering creativity.

Furthermore, the obsession with being busy can distract individuals from meaningful pursuits. Rather than prioritizing depth over breadth, many feel pressured to participate in numerous superficial activities in order to impress college admissions officers or future employers. This “resume-building” mentality, however, can prevent students from discovering their true passions or developing expertise in a particular area. It is far more worthwhile to engage deeply in a few activities that genuinely inspire growth, rather than spreading oneself thin across obligations that provide little intrinsic satisfaction.

Moreover, equating busyness with worth perpetuates a culture in which self-care and leisure are viewed as indulgences rather than necessities. In reality, downtime is not wasted time; it is an indispensable element of a healthy, fulfilling life. Moments of rest and recreation allow individuals to recharge, gain new perspectives, and form stronger relationships. By intentionally carving out time for reflection or relaxation, people can foster resilience and cultivate a more balanced, meaningful existence.

In conclusion, although being busy may initially seem like a prerequisite for success, it is ultimately a poor substitute for genuine fulfillment and well-being. Instead of idolizing packed schedules, we should recognize the value of balance, intentionality, and mindful engagement. Only then can we achieve not just outward accomplishments, but authentic satisfaction and personal growth.

In the twenty-first century, technology has become an inseparable facet of daily existence, shaping the ways in which we communicate, learn, and solve problems. Admittedly, technology is not without its drawbacks, such as concerns about privacy or the potential for social isolation. Technology has undeniably revolutionized nearly every aspect of our lives, weaving itself so thoroughly into the fabric of modern society that imagining a world without it feels almost impossible. In the past, high-quality education was often confined to the privileged few who could afford private tutors or expensive scholastic institutions. Furthermore, technology has revolutionized communication, shrinking our vast world and enabling connections that transcend geographical boundaries. Diseases that once decimated populations are now treatable or preventable, and medical information is more accessible than ever. While ethical dilemmas and concerns about data privacy must be addressed, the overall progress in health outcomes demonstrates the irreplaceable role of technology in modern medicine.

In conclusion, while technology is not without its flaws, its positive impact on education, communication, and healthcare is transformative and far-reaching. By embracing technological advancements and addressing their associated challenges thoughtfully, we can harness their potential to improve society and enrich our lives. Rejecting technology, therefore, means turning our backs on unparalleled opportunities for growth and progress.

Through unprecedented access to information, enhanced connectivity, and the facilitation of innovation, technology has not only transformed societies but also provided individuals with tools to improve their lives.

First and foremost, technology has revolutionized access to information. Where once knowledge was confined to libraries or the privileged few, the internet now democratizes learning for billions across the globe. For instance, students are no longer limited by the resources available in their local schools; instead, they can watch lectures from leading universities, participate in online forums, and access a vast array of digital textbooks. In the realm of business and science, cloud computing and collaborative tools have enabled multinational teams to tackle challenges ranging from climate change to medical research, achievements that would be unimaginable without technological integration.

Admittedly, there are legitimate concerns regarding the overuse of technology, such as cyberbullying, decreased attention spans, and privacy risks. However, these challenges are not inherent to technology itself but rather to its misuse. With effective education on digital citizenship and responsible use, society can mitigate these risks while continuing to reap the benefits.

In conclusion, although technology is not without its pitfalls, its overall impact on society is profoundly positive. By granting access to information, fostering global collaboration, and catalyzing innovation, technology empowers individuals and communities alike. Instead of fearing its influence, we should embrace technology’s possibilities, working collectively to harness its power for the greater good.

In the contemporary era, technology has permeated every facet of human existence, rapidly transforming the way we communicate, learn, and solve problems. While critics often express alarm over the potential negative consequences of technological progress—such as diminished attention spans, social isolation, or even job displacement—it is essential to recognize that, on balance, the impact of technology has been overwhelmingly positive. By fostering unprecedented access to information, enhancing productivity, and uniting people across vast distances, technology has become an indispensable force for good in society.

To begin with, technology has democratized access to knowledge in ways that would have been unimaginable merely a generation ago. Through the internet, individuals from virtually any background can access a plethora of educational resources, ranging from academic journals and online courses to instructional videos. This immense repository of information enables self-directed learning and empowers those who might otherwise be excluded from traditional educational opportunities. For instance, a high school student living in a rural community can now take advanced placement courses online or participate in virtual science competitions, leveling the academic playing field.

Moreover, technological advancements have revolutionized productivity in both personal and professional contexts. Automation and sophisticated software have streamlined previously laborious tasks, enabling individuals and organizations to accomplish far more in less time. In the medical field, for example, doctors now use artificial intelligence to analyze medical images with increased accuracy, leading to faster and more precise diagnoses. Similarly, businesses leverage technology to optimize supply chains and tailor services to individual customers, ultimately benefiting consumers through improved quality and efficiency.

Perhaps most importantly, technology has redefined the concept of human connection. While some lament the rise of social media and digital communication, it is undeniable that technology has allowed people to maintain relationships across continents, collaborate on global projects, and support one another in times of crisis. During the COVID-19 pandemic, for instance, video conferencing tools not only enabled remote education and work, but also helped sustain emotional bonds among families and friends separated by lockdowns.

Admittedly, technology is not without its drawbacks, and responsible usage is necessary to mitigate issues such as privacy concerns or online misinformation. Nevertheless, to focus solely on these challenges is to ignore the transformative benefits that technology brings to our lives. When harnessed wisely, technology elevates human potential, breaks down barriers, and fosters a more connected, informed, and vibrant society. For these reasons, the impact of technology should be celebrated and embraced as a vital force for progress.

In the contemporary era, technology’s pervasive influence has transformed nearly every aspect of human life, engendering both unprecedented opportunities and formidable challenges. Rather than resist technological advancement out of fear or nostalgia, society should embrace it thoughtfully, striving to mitigate its challenges while harnessing its immense potential. While it is tempting for skeptics to dwell on the potential pitfalls—rampant screen time, privacy concerns, or the alleged erosion of genuine human connection—one cannot ignore the transformative and overwhelmingly positive impact technology has wrought upon society. By revolutionizing education, advancing healthcare, and enhancing global communication, technology has become the cornerstone of progress, elevating human potential in unprecedented ways.

First and foremost, technology’s profound effect on education has democratized the acquisition of knowledge. Whereas once learning was confined to the four walls of a classroom or the pages of a textbook, students today can access a universe of information with a few keystrokes. Platforms such as Khan Academy, Coursera, and even YouTube provide free or affordable instruction in virtually every subject imaginable. With the aid of interactive simulations, virtual labs, and adaptive learning algorithms, students with diverse learning styles and abilities can thrive in ways that were previously unimaginable. Thus, rather than serving as a distraction, technology is more aptly viewed as a gateway to intellectual empowerment and academic equity.

Moreover, the advancements in healthcare attributable to technology are nothing short of miraculous. Through telemedicine, patients in remote areas can consult with specialists across the globe, bridging geographical divides that once dictated life or death. Cutting-edge medical devices, powered by artificial intelligence, enable earlier diagnoses and more precise treatments, vastly improving survival rates and quality of life. Electronic health records streamline care, reduce errors, and facilitate invaluable medical research. In these ways, technology not only alleviates suffering but also extends and enriches the lives of millions.

Finally, technology has woven a global tapestry of communication, fostering understanding and collaboration on a scale previously reserved for science fiction. Social networks, instant messaging, and video conferencing have made it possible for individuals to maintain relationships across continents and time zones. While some lament the superficiality of digital interactions, it is undeniable that technology has also empowered marginalized voices, sparked social movements, and enabled cultural exchange at an astonishing pace.

Admittedly, technology presents challenges that demand vigilance and thoughtful regulation. However, to dismiss its impact as predominantly negative is to disregard its capacity to enlighten minds, heal bodies, and unite humanity. Embracing technology’s potential while confronting its risks is not merely wise—it is imperative for a flourishing future.

Technology has become an inseparable part of modern society, fundamentally transforming the way individuals communicate, learn, and work. While critics often underscore its potential drawbacks—such as distractions, privacy concerns, or diminished face-to-face interaction—technology’s positive impact far outweighs its negatives. Through fostering global connectivity, enhancing educational opportunities, and streamlining everyday tasks, technology has significantly improved quality of life, and its continued advancement should be embraced rather than feared.

First and foremost, technology has redefined the boundaries of communication, allowing people to connect across continents in ways unimaginable just a few decades ago. Social media platforms, instant messaging, and video conferencing enable friends, families, and colleagues to interact in real time irrespective of geographical distance. This interconnectedness not only strengthens personal relationships but also promotes cross-cultural understanding. For instance, students can now collaborate on projects with peers around the globe, broadening their perspectives and preparing them for an increasingly interconnected world. While some argue that digital communication is less meaningful than in-person conversation, it offers invaluable inclusivity for those separated by distance or circumstance.

Moreover, technology has revolutionized the field of education, democratizing access to information and learning resources. Online courses, educational videos, and interactive software empower students to learn at their own pace and explore topics outside of a traditional classroom setting. Especially in underserved or remote communities, technology bridges educational gaps that would otherwise persist. During the COVID-19 pandemic, for example, digital tools enabled millions of students to continue their studies despite school closures. Critics might contend that technology causes distractions; however, when used judiciously, it can foster deeper engagement and understanding through personalized, adaptive learning experiences.

Finally, everyday life has become more efficient and convenient due to technological advancements. From automated banking and online shopping to smart home devices that regulate energy use, technology saves time and simplifies routine tasks. This efficiency not only increases productivity but also allows individuals to focus on creative pursuits and personal growth. While dependence on technology may create vulnerabilities—such as cybersecurity threats—these risks can be mitigated through responsible use and proactive safeguards.

In conclusion, although technology presents certain challenges that must not be ignored, its overall impact has been overwhelmingly positive. By enabling unprecedented communication, expanding educational horizons, and making daily life more manageable, technology continues to shape a brighter and more equitable future. Rather than resist these changes, society should harness technological innovations mindfully, ensuring that their benefits remain accessible to all.

One autumn morning, Charles Chaplin was taking his daily walk while smoking a cigar. At one moment , in one of the backstreets of his city,  he noticed an abandoned baby on the ground next to him, crying. After looking around and checking if it belonged to someone else , he took it in his arms. Then , he saw a lady with a baby carriage and another baby in it and Charles assumed that the baby he found belonged to her , too. Making that false connection , he approached her and said to her : '' Pardon me , you dropped something ''.  However, he quickly returned back to get the baby with him due to the fact that the lady was shouting that the baby was not hers. Upset as he was, he took the baby and left . Since he also wanted to get rid of it himself , he headed to the place where he found it so as to leave it there . By chance , at the moment that Charles was leaving the baby , a policeman appeared. He observed Charles and misinterpreted his moves to leave the baby on the ground as Charles seemed to be abandoning it himself . For that reason , as soon as Charles noticed the policeman , he took the baby with him again. Then , he also tried to leave the baby in the arms of another old man with the excuse that he wanted to tie his shoelaces. Since he managed to lead the old man on , Charles ran and found a hiding place . Given the fact that the old man wished to get rid of the baby too , he left it to the same lady that Charles had met earlier. Unfortunately for Charles, this lady , after coming out of a shoe store and noticing that she had two babies again, when she spotted Charles wandering around carelessly, she attacked him . Then , she forcibly took him to the baby carriage and nervously explained to him again that the baby was not hers. While those two were fighting , the madame called out to the policeman to put the situation in order.  After Charles was given back the baby by the lady, he walked away and began to make some thoughts about how to deal with that whole thing. At that time , his cane fell and he sat down on the edge of the pavement to rest and continue his thoughts while cuddling the baby. At that moment , when he was tidying the baby's coverings , he found a note between them. He opened it and the following were written : '' Please love and care for this orhan child ''. Understanding the note , Charles hid it in his coat , took the baby into his arms and since then it became <<his baby>> !!!

A few moments later he finds a little baby, wrapped in blankets laying on the ground in a little corner. Chaplin looks up to the balcony which causes for comedic effect. He seems to think that someone had thrown out their child, just like they are throwing out trash out of the balcony. He immediatly takes the baby and tries to find it's mother or father. He tries to find someone he can give the baby to. After seeing a woman with a stroller, he assumes that the baby belongs to the woman eventhough there was already a baby in the stroller. He carefully places the child in the stroller and tries to get out of the situation as politely as possible. He even says: ''you dropped something'' and is tipping his head, like he has done something admirable. But the woman is not accepting that child and gives the baby back to Chaplin. His second idea is to simply place the baby back to where he found it. On the street, right next to a trash can. This plan is quickly interrupted by the police officer passing by. He is not asking why Chaplin is trying to put the baby there, he simply gives him a look and Chaplin takes the baby and goes away. His third plan is to give the baby to a man he sees on the street. He told the man that he has to tie his shoe and as soon as the man takes the child, Chaplin runs away. The man, holding the baby in his arms sees the woman with the stroller and, like Chaplin just places the baby in the stroller and runs away. Chaplin is passing by that woman a moment later and she immediatly recognizes him and is furious. She even smacks him on his head and gives him back the baby. In that moment, the tramp was visibly desperate. He did not know what to do with the baby so he just sat on the sidewalk. Unwrapping the child a little bit, he discovers a note that says ''love this orphan child''. After that he gives the baby a little hug, gets up and goes his own way. He is not looking for anyone he can give the baby anymore. He is taking it with him.

Little John had a little frog and a little dog named Jonny Jr. Little JOhn and Jonny Jr. were very happy about the little frog. They put it in a glas and looked at it every evening. "Where has our little frog gone?", he asked his dog Jonny Jr., who did not have an answer to this question.    So John and Jonny Jr. went into the forest to look after the frog. They looked behind every tree and every stone, little John even climbed a small hill so that he could look deeper into the forest in search for the frog. Then, suddenly and without prior notice a deer came along and threw John and Jonny Jr. into a lake. The water of the lake was very cold so little John was a bit angry about being thrown into it at first.     But after a while, little John saw that the frog he was searching for lived inside the lake happily together with his family. John and Jonny Jr. decided that the best decision was to leave the frog at the place where he belongs -together with his family at the lake. Last weekend I watched a movie called "Scream".  It is a thriller/horror film that happens around the 90's. The protagonist of this story is Sydney Prescott. He wears a black robe and a white mask with black eyes and a wide mouth. Due to his appearance, news reporters and people call him "ghostface". And now ghostface is trying to hunt Sydney down. She's able to find shelter in her boyfriend, Billie loomies, and his friends. One day Sydney's friends decide to throw a party in honor of those students who have passed away by the hands of ghostface. To no one's surprise, ghostface attacks and kills almost everyone in this party, leaving only Sydney and his father (who up until this points, had been declared missing). And it is revealed that the killer was Sydney's boyfriend, Billie Loomis, this whole time. Sydney after finding this out runs and asks for help to one of his friends that was still alive, Stu Macher. But here's the sugar on the cream, as a plot twist, it is revealed that Stu is, in fact, also the killer and has teamed up with Billie to commit all this horrible acts. Billie reveals his motivations of why is he trying to kill Sydney. He says that Sydney's mother had an affair with Billie's father, what caused Billie's mother to abandon them. Stu , on the other hand, was just crazy and wanted to be a famous serial killer. Luckily, they weren't able to outsmart Sydney, and they were both killed, putting an end to all the unnecessary violence.

In the short film the protagonist, Charlie Chaplin, takes a walk in a seemingly run-down neighbourhood. Just as he attempts to light a cigar, he sees a baby wrapped up in a blanket, lying on the ground next to a metal container.    He picks the baby up and attempts to find out where it came from and how it got there. As he turns around he sees a woman with a child sitting in a pram and figures that the baby must belong to her. He puts it into the pram, but the woman insists that the baby is not hers.   Chaplin then takes the child back and goes to put it back down where he found it. However, he is interrupted by a police officer patrolling in the neighbourhood, so he quickly picks the baby back up and leaves.   Just as he rounds the corner he sees an elderly man and gives the baby to him, pretending to tie his shoelaces before running the other way. The elderly man attempts to run after him, but instead spots the same pram Chaplin put the baby into before. The man repeats Chaplin's previous action and leaves as well.   In that moment, the protagonist comes out of hiding and happens to pass that same pram. The woman Chaplin met earlier spots him and beats him with an umbrella, thinking that it was him who put the baby back. They argue and as the same police officer form earlier joins them Chaplin takes the child out of the pram.   Defeated, he sits down on the pavement. As he touches the baby's blanket he feels the hard edge of a note and takes it out. It says: "Please love and care for this orphan child". Chaplin's heart seems to melt reading this and so he accepts his new role as adoptive father, gets up and leaves with the child in his arms.

I have recently watched a movie called "The Mist". It's about a group of people trapped in a supermarket when a mysterious mist filled with deadly creatures rolls into town. The ending is really messed up because, after losing all hope, the protagonist and his family would rather die on their own terms than be slaughtered by these creatures. He kills his family with a gun, but he runs out of bullets, so he walks towards the mist waiting for his fate to be sealed, but then he hears sirens, he sees trucks and tanks coming out of the mist, it was the army, they managed to kill the creatures and rescue many survivors. The protagonist couldn't believe it, he killed his entire family, and it was all for nothing. Truly a gut wrenching story.

The Thirteen Roses (Las Trece Rosas) is a historical drama  about the fight, imprisonment and later execution of thirteen republican women during the first year of the Spanish post-war period. After having won the Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) Francisco Franco established a dictatorship, in which the first years were tremendously violent for those who didn't share his ideologies. Nevertheless none of these facts could discourage the thirteen roses, who fought for their beliefs until the very end of their lives.      Despite their young  age - most of them were under-age, these women fought and worked secretly for the reinstatement of the Spanish Republic and against the dictatorship regime recently set up. They were therefore arrested and jailed and it is during the wait for their trial that they all meet. Meanwhile a macabre process of interrogation in which human rights are clearly attacked takes place. Moreover the vulnerability of life during these periods is clarified; one of the prosecuted is a fascist side defender but faces the same destiny after being caught hiding at her home a friend of hers, a sympathizer of the Republic.      The movie is a representation of one of the darkest chapters in Spanish history. It is a moving story that brings the onlooker to tears, feeling anger, impotence and hence making it impossible not to  put themselves in the protagonists' shoes. In my opinion, a marvellous film.

The last movie I saw was Bad Samaritan, it is a suspense movie that keeps the viewer constantly on alert. I enjoyed it a lot on the one hand, for the performance of the characters and the atmosphere created in the film and, on the other hand, for the story, different from previous films that I had seen. At the beginning of the video, Charlie Chaplin is walking up the street; he then enters an alley and stops to light up a cigar. After that, he takes off his gloves and throws them in the bin next to him. When he hears a cry, he turns around and finds a baby wrapped up in a blanket and lying on the ground. He picks him up and starts looking for his parents: he spots a woman walking by with a stroller and reaches her up to put the baby in the stroller while she is away. However, when she comes back, he has to get the baby back because the lady does not seem to be his mother. Mr. Chaplin then decides to put him where he found him, but as soon as he stands up and turns around, he finds a police officer harshly looking at him; he understands he has no choice but to keep the baby, and simply goes away while holding him. While walking, he passes next to a man and, with the excuse to fix his shoe, he gives the baby to the man and runs away to hide somewhere. Not being able to find Mr. Chaplin, the other man puts the baby in the first stroller he sees, which also happens to belong to the woman that the protagonist met before. In the meantime, Mr. Chaplin comes out from where he was hiding and walks up the street where the woman is. As soon as she spots him, she runs up to him and beats him up with an umbrella, angry because she believes that he is the one that put the baby into the stroller once again. Mr. Chaplin tries to justify himself, but the same police officer he met before arrives and severely looks at him once more. At that point, the protagonist gives up and takes the baby away with him. He then sits on a sidewalk and lifts a manhole cover, probably thinking of putting the baby inside; eventually he changes his mind and closes it. While looking at the baby, he finds a sheet of paper in the baby’s blanket, which asks to take care of that orphan. In the end, Mr. Chaplin gently smiles at the baby and says something to him, while getting up and walking away holding him in his arms.

I haven't watched any film recently, to be honest, so I guess I will write about one of my favourite ones: Scott Pilgrim Vs The World.   This film, by Edward Wright, is based on its namesake comic books, and it is styled to look like it is a comic book which is also styled to look like a video game. Pretty meta, I would say.   Our protagonist, Scott Pilgrim, is the bassist of a local band, trying to get over his successful ex-girlfriend by dating a Chinese high-schooler. This quickly goes sour when he meets his love interest, Ramona Flowers, the new girl in town who is running away from her troubled past and decides to juggle dating both girls.   If that was not hard enough, Ramona comes with baggage: seven evil ex-boyfriends who Scott will have to defeat in order to get his happily ever after.

"Harry Potter and te Prisoner of Azkaban" is the third installment of the Harry Potter series, where Harry faces new challenges and uncovers deep secrets about his past. The story begins with Harry returning to Hogwarts for his third year, only to learn that Sirius Black, a dangerous prisoner form Azkaban, has escaped and is allegedly after him.        As the plot progresses, Harry discovers that Sirius Black is not the villain everyone believes him to be. With the help of his friends Ron and Hermione, Harry learns that Sirius is, in fact, his godfather and that he was wrongly accused of betraying Harry's parents to Lord Voldemort. The true traitor is Peter Pettigrew, who has been hiding in plain sight as Ron’s pet rat, Scabbers.        One of the most significant elements of the film is the introduction of the Dementors, terrifying creatures that guard Azkaban. These creatures feed on despair and fear, which makes them a constant threat to Harry, who struggles to defend himself from their effects.        The film also explores the importance of friendship and loyalty, as Harry, Ron, and Hermione work together to uncover the truth. Ultimately, "The Prisoner of Azkaban" is a tale of discovering who you truly are and the value of trust, even when things aren’t as they first appear.

For as long as I can remember, a couple of weeks ago, I saw one of the films that has most made me reflect on my life. It is the film 'Los renglones torcidos de Dios', a psychological thriller by Oriol Paulo. This is one of those films that grabs you from minute one and leaves you in suspense until the end.    As for the plot, it is a Spanish film based on the investigation of a detective who goes into a sanatorium in order to solve a supposed murder case. The plot of the film is based on the different investigations of the main character, while unexpected events unfold during her stay in the sanatorium.    Both the main character and the rest of the cast are very professional Spanish actors and actresses, which makes the film so real and so good. In addition, many of them have starred in other thrillers which are also very interesting.    Definitely, this kind of background is the one that hooks you to sit in the cinema seat and enjoy it. Moreover, it is the kind of film that has a very open ending so that you can reflect on it and think a little further.

PRIDE AND PREJUDICE (2005)   Mr Bennet lived in the Longbourn estate in some place in the countryside of England with his wife and five daughters. Mrs Bennet wanted all her daughters well married as a life goal. That dream started to take form when the Netherfield manor is bought by a misterious wealthy man.    This man, Mr. Bingley, along with his sister and best friend Mr. Darcy, make their first public appearance in a ball. On this event Mr Bingley and the eldest of the Bennet daughters, Jane, fall in love at first sight. On the other side, Mr Darcy and the second eldest daughter of the Bennets, Elisabeth, make themselves acquainted but start off on the wrong foot.   Mr Collins, a cousin of the Bennets, and heir to the Longbourn estate, is recommended by Lady Catherine the Bourgh, one of the wealthiest people in the whole England, to marry so that he shall live as a priest. He thinks of Jane to do so, but Mrs Bennte refuses and offers Elisabeth in her place. She refuses this offering, and Mr Collins flees in shame. He then marries Elisabeth's best friend, Charlotte Lucas.   Later on, there is a ball in Netherfield in which Elisabeth and Mr Darcy dance and have the chance to talk. In this ball, Mrs Bennet declares her intentions to marry all her daughters well to wealthy men. Mr Darcy hears this and advises his friend to stop pursuing Jane, and they return to London.    In the spring, Jane visits Charlotte, in Kent, and they are all invited to Rosings Park, Lady Catherine's home. There are also Mr Darcy and his cousin, Colonel Fitzwilliam and the later tells Elisabeth how Mr Darcy saved a friend from a disadvantageous marriage, and she realises that is Jane's which he is talking about. Mr Darcy proposes to Elisabeth, and she refuses because he has heard some dark stories about himb from George Wickham, a charming officer who the Bennets met previously in town.   Mr Darcy sends her a letter in which he explains that he saved her sister from marrying Mr Wickham, who is revealed to be a gambler in need of money.  He also explains why he did separate Jane and Mr Bingley, and Jane fells shame for her family and for her prejudice of Mr Darcy.    Later that year, Jane visits Pemberley, Mr Darcy's estate. There Mr Darcy and Jane meet, and he is sweet and kind with Elisabeth and her company. But there Jane is told that Lydia, one of her younger sisters, has run off with Mr Wickham. Although Mr Darcy wants this to be kept in secret, Elisabeth gets to know that Mr Darcy was the one responsible of convincing Mr Wickham to marry and for paying the wedding with all its costs.    Mr. Bingley and Mr. Darcy return to Netherfield. Jane accepts Mr. Bingley's proposal. Later Lady Catherine tries to make Elisabeth promise never to marry Mr Darcy, who is promised to her daughter Anne. But she refuses to do so, and Mr Darcy, motivated by this, proposes to Elisabeth and she accepts.

A nameless man, played by Charles Chaplin, walks through an alley and is first almost and then properly hit by objects thrown from windows (presumably debris and rubbish). The text "Awkward Ass" is inserted. Following that, he lights a cigarette and, shortly after, discovers a seemingly abandoned baby wrapped in a blanket lying on the ground. He looks up as if he assumes the baby was thrown from the window like the rubbish before. He picks the baby up, continuing to look around to figure out where it came from. Then, he sees a woman with a pram and puts the baby in said pram; the text "Pardon me, you dropped something" is inserted. The woman, however, does not want the baby as it is not hers, and Chaplin's character is forced to take it with him. He tries to put the baby back where he found it, but a police officer stops him from following through with that plan, and he is once more forced to keep the baby. In his next attempt to get rid of it, he hands it to a man with a cane and runs off. The man follows Chaplin's character, but cannot catch up with him. The man with the cane walks past the same pram in which Chaplin's character dropped the baby and proceeds to do the same, after which he runs off. Shortly after, Chaplin's character passes the pram; at the same time, the mother leaves the shop the pram is parked in front of. She assumes that Chaplin's character put the baby in her pram again and beats him with her umbrella several times before she forces him (with the help of the aforementioned police officer) to take the baby with him. Chaplin's character sits down on the kerb with the baby, opens the gully for a short moment as if he considers throwing the baby in, and then finds a note in the baby's blanket that reads "Please love and care for this orphan child". He smiles at the baby, they stand up, and walk away.

American Fiction is about a professor and writer who tries so hard to not to be defined as an African-American writer, only to find himself somewhat coerced by the publishing industry to do it. As a manner of a convoluted prank, he writes, under a pseudonym, the most stereotypical African American story he can think of, only to find out his work is a complete success. Meanwhile he embarks in a personal trip of reconsidering his relationship with his family, with a new lover, and the very stereotype he tries to fight.

One day, at the fair in my town, I got a frog at one of the games. I was very excited as it was an act of responsibility and maturity that my parents had entrusted to me. Therefore, elated, I named her Tiana and then went to sleep to discuss it the next day with my friends from school.   However, when I woke up the next day ... the frog was missing! I forgot to change it to another container and it must have jumped out of the boat ... So I decided to go with my dog ​​Jack to look for it in the woods. Jack and I yelled and yelled, respectively, for Tiana's name, but we got no response or croaking whatsoever. I even climbed the trees and rocks that overlooked the river, but Tiana wasn't there.   Suddenly, I felt like something grabbed my shirt and lifted me up. Was she flying or was she still dreaming? I turned around and saw it. A deer was picking me up off the ground! Jack came to my aid but the deer threw us both into the river. Good thing we were not in the depths of the river and, luckily, we fell on a log. To our surprise, Tina was there! And with her whole family!   There I understood that, like me, Tina had a family to take care of her and that taking her away from them would not achieve anything. So Jack and I went home ecstatic with happiness that we had helped our friend Tina.

One film that I have recently saw is "Us". This is a horror and suspense film. It is about a girl in the 80s (her name is Adelaide) who was with her parents in a fun fair; suddenly, she leaves and goes to an attraction that was closed where there were a lot of mirrors. She sees herself but it is not her reflection, it is a real person; this person seizes her by the neck. Because of that, Adelaide was traumatized and could not speak for a long time. Then, we see that she has grown up and now she has a family with two kids and that they are going to that same place, to have some relaxation in a stunning house.   When they have been settled, exactly at 11:11 p.m, they see a family holding hands being without moving in front of their house. They get scared since they do not speak and they do not answer their question about who they are. Suddendly, they start moving and they enter the house, and it turns out that that family was the family of Adelaide, but wearing  red overalls. The woman starts speaking with a very harsh voice saying that she had a hard life while Adelaide had a perfect life. Each member of the family tries to kill each döppelganger until they succeed. Later on, they find that this did not happen exclusively to them but that the whole world is suffering this as well. The family goes back to their home but in a moment, the döppelganger of Adelaide, who was not dead in reality, kidnaps her son. Adelaide (the good one) has to follow them until she arrives to the attraction that left her with a trauma. There, she finds the bad Adelaide who explains her that that people wearing overalls are clones created by the government to control people in the world. She also told her that when that experiment failled, they were abandoned underground for generations imitating the actions of the people that lived in the world. Then, they start fighting and the good Adelaide finally kills the bad one and gets his son.   In the last scene of the film we see the entire family in an ambulance. Adelaide starts remembering the night of the fun fair and we get to know that her döppelganger asphyxiated the original one until she left her unconscious (hurting her throat (that is why she had a harsh voice)), tied her in the underground world and adapted herself to the normal world, so now the Adelaide we see at the end of the film was in reality the original Adelaide that was asphyxiated, therefore, the family was living with the clone from the underground this whole time until now.

Mr. Charplin is walking through a dirty alley when he notices a baby by the dumpster. At first, he believes it to belong to a lady pushing a baby carriage nearby, so he tries to sneak it into her carriage. He is scolded and told to take the unknown baby back. He then tries to leave the baby where he had originally found it, but a police officer nearby makes him pick it up, as to not seem like he himself was leaving his own baby there. While walking away, he asks an elderly man if he could hold the baby while he cleans his shoes – and promptly runs away. The elderly man follows him, but loses his track and bumps into the same lady as mr. Chaplin before. He, as well, leaves the baby in her carriage she left outside the shop. When she comes out, mr. Chaplin happens to be passing by. She recognizes him and the baby, so she gets angry at him for having tried the same trick twice. Because the police officer is also following him, having seen him suddenly babyless, mr. Chaplin is pressured to again take the baby. After that, he sits by the road and notices a note in the baby's blanket. The baby is an orphan and whoever left him there asks the finder to take care of her. That's when mr. Chaplin decides to keep the baby.

Last film I have recently watched is the well known "Society of the Snow" directed by Bayona. It is a "re-make" of the original one "Alive" I also watched when I was a teenager (not sure exactly how old I was but I know it did an impact on my perception of flights and travels).    The film tells the story of a plane crush in Los Andes. A group of rugby players were flying from Montevideo (Uruguay) to Santiago de Chile (Chile) when they suddenly found themselves stuck in the middle of nowhere and surrounded by kilometres of snow. Only sixteen out of more than fourty passengers survived during more than seventy days having no food, no appropiate clothes for the under-zero-temperatures and only part of the plane to hide themselves from snow storms, wind and cold.   The group of teens kept waiting to be rescued during eleven days until they knew from a radio station that searching works have ceased and they would be restarted at the end of November (as soon as ice started melting).    This seems to be the most importante part of the film as it is exactly when the group start acting, doing, thinking and rising from scratch.   After several failed attempts to reach, firstly the other part of the crushed plane to fix the radio and ask for help and, finally find a way out of that endless frozen dessert, two of them started a ten-day journey through Los Andes until they reached civilization and ware able to ask for help.   The film is about the importance of self improvement, of working together as a team and above all the never give up feeling

I am not really into watching TV but last week I saw a movie tha blew my mind. The Truman Show tells the story of a man whose life is a TV show. The point of it all is that he does not know that everything that happens to him is staged and televised. Where he lives is a film studio and all the people surrounding him are actors.    He lives like this for a long time until one day he starts noticing that something fishy was going on. Then he does everthing he can to try and improvise what he does to see what is the reaction of the people and confirm his suspicions. The movie comes with a deep phylosophical component because the people controlling it all were willing to do a lot of morally gray things to make Truman`s life stay the same so they could keep making money. The film ends when he realises it is all a lie and he choses to escape from it by leaving the studio.

Once upon a time a boy named Timmy and his dog called Spencer were best friends. Together they had many adventures. One day they caught a frog and put it inside a glass. They watched it all evening. It was very fascinating. When it was time to go to sleep, they both went to bed, while the frog was still inside the glass in front of the bed. In the night the frog crawled out of its glass. The next morning Timmy and Spencer woke up and were excited to play with the frog, but unfortunately they found an empty glass. Hence they immediately went outside to look for the missing frog. Timmy and Spencer went out into the woods, climbed onto trees and rocks, however they couldn't find the frog. As Timmy and Spencer climbed onto a rock, a deer suddenly appeared and shoved them into a little pond. Luckily they were able to swim and got out of the water. Thereupon they climbed onto a trunk of a tree. When they heard a sound that seemed to be the croaking of frogs, they looked behind the trunk. Surprisingly they discovered the frog and its family! Afterwards they all played together all day long and had lots of fun!

One of my favorite movies is "Love, Simon". It tells the story of Simon, a gay boy who is afraid to come out because he doesn't know if people will stop thinking it's him. Undoubtedly, watching this movie makes you think and realize the respect we should give to people. That is why this is one of my favorite movies, and, for that reason, I recommend it.

Charlie Chaplin is walking along a street, smoking a cigar. A woman throws something out of the window, but Charlie walks on unconcerned. Farther into the lane he finally gets hit by some rubbish someone threw out of the window. Hence his cigar fell on the ground, he takes a new one out of his tiny metal box, which he carries in his chest pocket. When he throws his gloves away he suddenly sees a baby lying on the ground between the rubbish. He picks the baby up and tries to pass it to a woman with a carriage, that randomly passes by. Since the baby does not belong to her, she forces Charlie to take it back. Charlie does not know what to do with the baby, so he tries to put it back where he found it. Unfortunately, a policeman comes by so Charlie is not able to drop it in the lane. Ich tricks an old man into holding the baby and runs away. The man leaves the baby in the woman’s carriage, who is nor amused about it and thinks Charlie left it there. As he passes by the woman forces him to take the baby with him by calling for the policeman. Charlie is clueless about what to do with the baby, so he sits down on the sidewalk and while playing around he finds a letter, that says “please love and care for this orphan child”. Charlie is affected by the letter and decides to care for the baby.

I had to see "Hard Times" by Charles Dickens because i had an oral exam of it this semester. The movie is related to industralization in England, around XIX century. On one hand It talks about a family that educates their children based on logical and rational facts.  The children finally grew up with no sense of imagination or creativity, that-s bad for them because it makes them choose bad decisions in life like being married with no love or being a thief with no hope. On another hand there are other topics that talks about how rich people didn-t take care about their poor workers lifestyle or needs. All topics on this movie made me think about how we are still stuck into this materialistic society where money and power means happiness. Maybe the only thing that we have now to change this old system is the education and knowledge; those will be our "guns" to face this order based on consuming and buying.

"How to Lose a Guy in ten days"    This film is a romcom that tells the history of a unexpected couple. On the one hand, we have Andie Anderson, a writer in a women’s magazine who is tasked with the unusual assignment of writing an article on how to put a man off in ten days. To write this article she must date someone and make all the classic "mistakes" women used to make in relationships. On the other hand, Benjamin, an enterprising businessman, makes a bet with his boss that he can make any woman fall in love with him, if he is able to win the wage he will run a huge campaign.   No matter each other's reasons, Andie and Ben start dating, each determined to succeed in their respective challenges. Meanwhile, loads of bizarre encounters as Andie goes out of her way to sabotage her relation with Ben.   As the experiment goes on, strong feelings begin to emerge, complicating their plans. Finally, both Andie and Ben must admit their true feelings and admite that their love is real and no just a consecuence of a bet or a job.

Finally, he smiles at the baby and leaves, still holding it in his arms.

The short video clip titled "The Kid" shows Chaplin, the protagonist, walking through an alley. After narrowly avoiding being covered in garbage thrown out of windows, and after lighting a cigarrette, Chaplin finds an orphaned baby lying next to a garbage can. He picks it up and decides to return it. A mother walks by, pushing her own baby in a baby carriage. Chaplin slips the orphan into the carriage next to the other baby, but the mother notices and protests until Chaplin takes back the orphan. Discouraged, Chaplin plans to place the baby back next to the garbage can where he found it. A stern-looking police officer observes him, which causes Chaplin to change his mind and keep walking with the baby. Around the corner, he sees another passer-by and hands him the orphan. Chaplin runs off and hides, and the confused passer-by, seeking to get rid of the orphan as well, places the baby in the mother's baby carriage. Chaplin, who is now convinced that the matter is resolved, happens to walk by the baby carriage, and the mother suspects him of having slipped the orphan into her carriage a second time. The police officer joins the altercation, and the mother protests to the officer, so that poor Chaplin is forced to take back the orphan yet again. Exhausted, Chaplin sits down on the curb with the orphan in his arm. He finds a note which asks the person who finds the orphan to take loving care of the baby. Chaplin smiles affectionately and appears to accept that responsibility. He stands up, with the baby still in his arms, and walks off scene.

The abrupt and ubiquitous shift to remote instruction during the recent global crisis, while initially daunting, has undeniably recalibrated our understanding of pedagogical efficacy. Far from being a mere temporary expedient, distance learning, when thoughtfully implemented, presents a versatile and indispensable component of modern education, offering unique advantages that warrant its continued integration into our academic frameworks.

One of the most compelling arguments for distance learning resides in its unparalleled flexibility and accessibility. For students grappling with diverse learning styles, geographical barriers, or demanding extracurricular commitments, asynchronous learning platforms provide the autonomy to pace oneself, delve deeper into areas of intrinsic interest, and review complex material as needed. This self-directed approach, which empowers learners to manage their schedules and cultivate independent study habits, is a crucial prerequisite for success in higher education and the professional world. Furthermore, it democratizes access to specialized courses and expert instructors that might otherwise be unavailable in a traditional school setting, thereby enriching the educational landscape for a broader student body.

While critics often highlight the potential for social isolation or a perceived lack of immediate teacher interaction, these very challenges, when approached with proactive strategies and robust support systems, can foster invaluable 21st-century skills. Navigating virtual collaborative projects, for instance, cultivates digital literacy, effective online communication, and problem-solving, all of which are increasingly vital in a globally connected society. Moreover, the necessity of proactive engagement in an online environment galvanizes students to advocate for their own learning, articulating questions and seeking resources with greater independence. Consequently, rather than viewing these as inherent flaws, we should recognize them as opportunities to cultivate resilience and self-reliance within a structured, albeit virtual, environment.

Ultimately, distance learning is not a panacea, nor should it entirely supplant traditional classroom experiences. However, to dismiss its profound potential would be shortsighted. Furthermore, the inherent flexibility of asynchronous learning environments allows individuals to tailor their educational schedules around disparate life commitments, transforming what was once an exclusive pursuit into an attainable endeavor for a broader spectrum of learners.

Beyond these pragmatic benefits, remote education intrinsically cultivates a suite of crucial competencies indispensable for success in the twenty-first century. Navigating virtual learning platforms, managing project deadlines independently, and engaging in online collaborative endeavors all demand a heightened sense of self-discipline, time management, and intrinsic motivation. Consequently, students develop a robust autonomy and digital fluency, skills that extend far beyond the academic realm, preparing them for a professional landscape increasingly defined by remote work and digital interaction. Moreover, the ability to revisit lectures and resources ad infinitum empowers learners to optimize their cognitive engagement, fostering a deeper understanding that often eludes the one-time delivery of a traditional classroom.

In conclusion, while the initial foray into widespread distance learning exposed areas ripe for improvement, dismissing its long-term viability would be a significant oversight. Recognizing its immense potential to enhance accessibility, cultivate essential modern skills, and offer a more personalized learning experience, educators and policymakers must continue to refine and strategically incorporate distance learning. Ultimately, by embracing this evolving pedagogical paradigm, we can forge a more adaptable, inclusive, and effective educational system prepared to meet the demands of a dynamic global society.

The landscape of education has undergone a profound transformation, with distance learning emerging as a ubiquitous and increasingly indispensable component of modern pedagogy. While its rapid proliferation, particularly in recent years, undeniably offers a myriad of advantages, ranging from unparalleled flexibility to expanded access, a truly comprehensive assessment reveals that this model, though potent, is not a universal panacea and warrants judicious implementation to maximize its benefits while mitigating its inherent challenges.

One of the most compelling arguments for distance learning lies in its capacity to democratize education, transcending geographical barriers and offering bespoke learning pathways. For students who thrive on autonomy and self-paced study, or those grappling with scheduling conflicts due to extracurricular commitments or part-time jobs, the virtual classroom provides an invaluable avenue for academic continuity and personalized advancement. Furthermore, it enables access to specialized courses or expert instructors who might otherwise be geographically inaccessible, thereby enriching the educational experience significantly and fostering a deeper engagement with diverse subjects. The very nature of online resources, meticulously curated and readily available, empowers students to revisit complex topics as needed, cultivating a more profound understanding at their own rhythm.

However, despite these compelling benefits, the wholesale adoption of distance learning presents its own set of formidable obstacles. The absence of immediate, tangible social interaction, which is a cornerstone of the traditional classroom, can impede the development of crucial interpersonal skills and a sense of community. Collaborative projects, impromptu discussions, and the subtle cues of non-verbal communication that enrich in-person learning are often diminished in a virtual environment, potentially leading to feelings of isolation and disengagement for some learners. Moreover, the digital divide, where disparities in reliable internet access and suitable technology persist, underscores an equity issue that, if unaddressed, could exacerbate existing educational inequalities, leaving already vulnerable student populations further marginalized.

Ultimately, distance learning represents a powerful tool that, when thoughtfully integrated, can significantly enhance the educational journey. Recognizing its transformative potential, educators must also acknowledge its limitations, particularly concerning social development and equitable access. A hybrid model, which judiciously blends the flexibility of virtual platforms with the irreplaceable human connection and collaborative environment of physical classrooms, likely offers the most robust and holistic approach, ensuring that all students can flourish in an increasingly complex academic world.

Distance learning is a big topic many students talk about nowadays. For me, I think that go to school in person is much better than learn from home. There are many reasons why I feel this way, and I want to share them.

First, I miss my friends a lot when I learn at home. School is not only about books and lessons. It is also about people and connection. I like to see my friends every day. Second, the learning environment at home is not good for me. My house has many distractions my little brother plays games or my mom watches TV loudly. It is very hard to focus on my lesson when there is so much noise and activity around. In school, everyone is there to study. The classroom is quiet, and the teacher is right there in front of us. If I have a question, I can raise my hand and the teacher helps me immediately. Online, I must type a question or wait for my turn, which takes more time. Sometimes, my internet connection is bad, and I cannot hear the teacher clearly. This makes learning very difficult, and I cannot catch up easily. School is a place for learning, and home is a place for relax.

Third, distance learning often makes me lazy. It is hard to wake up early and start the day when I am at home, I can stay in my pajamas all day.
